content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To    : The File                                    Date  : March 28, 1980

From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        John Giansello

Subject: Opinion Request of United Professor of California
         dated March 14, 1980

Our advice given in response to this request is limited to
sponsoring organizations and their political action committees
eligible to file and filing in accordance with 2 Cal. Admin. Code
§1841 9.

The mailing in this case includes a circular previously paid for
by the political action committee and reported or reportable by the
latter as a separate expenditure.

The union will be preparing the mailing, including the circular,
and paying the costs of sending the entire envelope of material.

The mailing costs (postage, employee time, envelopes, etc.) must
be allocated on some reasonable basis between the political material
and the non-political contents of the mailing. The mailing costs
attributable to the political material will be a non-monetary con-
tribution by the union to the political action committee. The
political action committee must report the receipt of such value as
a non-monetary contribution from the union.

If the union and the political action committee are filing ac-
cording to §18419, either may be deemed the sender and the name and
address of either may appear on the outside of the envelope to satisfy
the requirements of Govt. Code §84305.

Opinion Request Meeting Participants                    March 14, 1980


John Giancello
Mass Mailings


     The United Professors of California is a labor organization. It
sponsors a political action committee which solicits its funds directly
from individual members of the union. None of its funds come from the
union treasury.

     The union itself wishes to send out a mailing to all members on
insurance and pension matters. None of this material will involve
political purposes as we define them.

     The political action committee has prepared and paid for the
printing of a circular to oppose Proposition 9.

     The political action committee wishes the union to include a copy
of the political circular in each envelope mailed to members in connection with insurance and pension matters. The union is agreeable and
will pay for the envelopes and postage, as well as its own enclosures.

     Who is the sender? (If the entire mailing is counted, the union
will bear the major portion of the cost.)

     What is the mass mailing, the entire package or just the political
enclosure and any incremental postal charge attributable to the inclusion of the political material? Or should the cost of the envelopes
also be pro-rated by the relative length, volume or weight of the
contents?

     What must be on the outside of the envelope?

     Our requirements for mass mailings in Government Code §84305 apply
only to persons who make "expenditures." 2 Cal. Admin. Code §13225(c)(3)
talks about expenditures that are "reportable" by persons other than
those described in 18225(b) who make payment for communications which
expressly advocate the nomination, election or defeat of a clearly
identified candidate or candidates, or the qualification, passage or
defeat of a clearly identified ballot measure (our multi-purpose
committees). The union in this case would be such a multi-purpose
committee if it made such payments. By 13225(c)(3) the only parts of
the payments that are reportable by a multi-purpose committee are
those directly attributable to the political communication. Regular
ongoing business overhead which will be incurred in similar amounts

Opinion Request Meeting
Participants                                                                           March 14, 1980


regardless of the political communication is not part of the expendi-
ture for a multi-purpose committee.

     Here, if the enclosure makes the entire package political, the
union will be making an expenditure, and because it is paying the
larger portion of the whole the union will be the sender.

     If the insurance and pension materials, on the other hand, are
"regulary covering business overhead" - including envelop and postage -
then the union will be making no "expenditure." The only "expenditures"
made will be by the PAC, and the mass mailing requirements would seem
to apply only to the PAC and its costs. Even if there is an increments
postal charge attributable to the enclosure, paid by the union, the
PAC will be paying the larger portion of the political costs and will
be the sender.

sections.question: The union wishes to send a mailing to members on insurance and pension matters (non-political). The political action committee has prepared a circular opposing Proposition 9 and wishes to be included in the envelope. The union will pay for envelopes and postage. The questions are: (1) Who is the sender? (2) What is the mass mailing - the entire package or just the political enclosure and incremental postal charges, or should envelope costs be pro-rated? (3) What must appear on the outside of the envelope?
sections.conclusion: Mailing costs (postage, employee time, envelopes, etc.) must be allocated on a reasonable basis between political and non-political contents. Costs attributable to political material constitute a non-monetary contribution by the union to the PAC, which must be reported. Either the union or PAC may be deemed the sender if both are filing under §18419; either's name and address may appear on the envelope to satisfy Govt. Code §84305 requirements. Whether the union makes a reportable expenditure depends on whether the insurance/pension materials constitute 'regular ongoing business overhead' or whether the enclosure makes the entire package political.
sections.question_synthetic: When a union includes a political action committee's political circular in a non-political mailing to members and pays for postage and envelopes, how should mailing costs be allocated, who is the sender, and what must appear on the envelope under California's mass mailing disclosure requirements?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The mailing costs must be reasonably allocated between political and non-political content, with the union's cost share being a non-monetary contribution to the PAC. Either organization can be the sender if both file under §18419. Whether the union makes a reportable expenditure depends on whether the insurance and pension materials are regular business overhead or whether inclusion of the political circular makes the entire package subject to political disclosure requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a memorandum rather than traditional advice letter format. Contains detailed factual background and legal analysis. No explicit 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' headers, but question and conclusion are clearly identifiable in narrative form. The document addresses mass mailing requirements under Govt. Code §84305 and reporting requirements under Cal. Admin. Code §18419 and §18225.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The union wishes to send a mailing to members on insurance and pension matters (non-political). The political action committee has prepared a circular opposing Proposition 9 and wishes to be included in the envelope. The union will pay for envelopes and postage. The questions are: (1) Who is the sender? (2) What is the mass mailing - the entire package or just the political enclosure and incremental postal charges, or should envelope costs be pro-rated? (3) What must appear on the outside of the envelope?

CONCLUSION: Mailing costs (postage, employee time, envelopes, etc.) must be allocated on a reasonable basis between political and non-political contents. Costs attributable to political material constitute a non-monetary contribution by the union to the PAC, which must be reported. Either the union or PAC may be deemed the sender if both are filing under §18419; either's name and address may appear on the envelope to satisfy Govt. Code §84305 requirements. Whether the union makes a reportable expenditure depends on whether the insurance/pension materials constitute 'regular ongoing business overhead' or whether the enclosure makes the entire package political.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that when a labor union includes a political action committee's political circular opposing a ballot measure in a non-political mailing about insurance and pensions, mailing costs must be reasonably allocated between political and non-political content, with the union's allocated costs treated as a non-monetary contribution to the PAC.
